syntaxReference: List<String> list = new ArrayList<>(); list.add("hi"); String x = list.get(0);
Set<Integer> set = new HashSet<>(); set.add(5); boolean b = set.contains(5);
Map<String,Integer> m = new HashMap<>(); m.put("a", 1); int v = m.getOrDefault("a", 0);
Iterator<String> it = list.iterator(); while (it.hasNext()) { String s = it.next(); if (s.isEmpty()) it.remove(); }
commonPatterns: Frequency count: Map<String,Integer> freq = new HashMap<>(); for (String w : words) { freq.put(w, freq.getOrDefault(w, 0) + 1); }
Deduplicate while preserving first-seen order: Set<String> seen = new HashSet<>(); List<String> out = new ArrayList<>(); for (String s : input) { if (seen.add(s)) out.add(s); }
Group by key: Map<K,List<V>> g = new HashMap<>(); for (V v : data) { K k = keyOf(v); g.computeIfAbsent(k, x -> new ArrayList<>()).add(v); }
Invert map to values->set of keys: Map<V,Set<K>> inv = new HashMap<>(); for (Map.Entry<K,V> e : m.entrySet()) { inv.computeIfAbsent(e.getValue(), x -> new HashSet<>()).add(e.getKey()); }
Top-K with counts: use HashMap for counts then place entries into a PriorityQueue<Map.Entry<K,Integer>> ordered by value desc.
quickTips: Prefer entrySet when you need both keys and values to avoid extra lookups.
Always implement both equals and hashCode for custom key classes; use immutable fields for stability.
Initialize HashMap/HashSet with an estimated capacity to reduce resizing when processing large inputs.
